content:
Authentication

Backend Development

System Design Concepts

Web Development

Multifactorauthentication

20 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Modern authentication solutions integrate biometrics, multi-factor authentication (MFA), and intelligent identity verification to deliver secure, frictionless access while protecting users against credential theft and unauthorized access. Image Credit: Cybernews — Best Authentication SolutionsModern applications no longer run as single servers with a database and a login form. They run as distributed fleets of services spread across regions, talking to each other over untrusted networks, serving traffic that never stops. In that world, authentication isn’t a password check — it’s the security perimeter the rest of the system leans on. It has to resist distributed brute-force attacks, manage stateless sessions across dozens of independent services, stay consistent under load, and still feel instant to a legitimate user typing in their password at 2 a.m.
Treating authentication as an afterthought — a CRUD module bolted onto a web framework — is one of the costliest architectural mistakes a team can make. A production-grade identity platform deserves the same rigor as a payments system: isolation, clear boundaries, defense in depth, and cryptography that’s actually been vetted.
This article walks through that platform end to end — architecture, data model, threat landscape, and implementation — building toward a system you could put in front of real users.
Contents

• 15 Mistakes That Keep Happening

1. Why Authentication Is Hard
Authentication is hard because it sits at the intersection of several disciplines at once: cryptography, network security, distributed state, and database design. Get any one of them wrong and the whole system is only as strong as its weakest layer.
Identity is more than a password
Verifying “who is this?” in production means reading context, not just credentials:

• Device fingerprinting — is this a browser and OS the account has seen before?

• IP intelligence and geolocation — is the request coming from a known VPN, or from Tokyo ten minutes after a login from London?

• Network behavior — are hundreds of failed attempts converging from the same CIDR block?
Evaluating all of this at line rate, without adding latency for the 99.9% of requests that are perfectly legitimate, is a caching and stream-processing problem as much as a security one.
Security and friction pull in opposite directions
Every security control — MFA prompts, short session lifetimes, device verification — adds friction, and friction costs conversions and support tickets. Remove the friction and you remove the protection. The way out is adaptive authentication: assess risk continuously and only escalate (step-up MFA, re-verification) when something looks anomalous, rather than applying maximum friction to every user all the time.
Identity has a lifecycle, not just a state

Press enter or click to view image in full size

An account moves through distinct, auditable states, and each transition needs its own validation:
Modeling this cleanly requires real transactional boundaries and a way to invalidate sessions immediately when state changes — not just on the next token expiry.
Why authentication deserves its own service
Embedding auth logic inside every service that needs it seems convenient, but it multiplies risk:

• Secret sprawl — every service ends up holding database credentials and signing keys it doesn’t strictly need.

• Coordinated upgrades — rotating a hashing algorithm or changing the session schema means redeploying the whole fleet at once.

• More doors to pick — every service that touches auth logic is a place a bug can turn into a bypass.
Isolating authentication into its own microservice lets you lock it down independently: a private VPC subnet, a database connection pool tuned for identity tables, and the ability to scale the auth layer without scaling everything else with it.
2. Authentication vs. Authorization
These two get conflated constantly, and the confusion is where a lot of real vulnerabilities live.

• Authentication (AuthN) answers “who are you?” — verifying a password, an OTP, a signature, or a token.

• Authorization (AuthZ) answers “what are you allowed to do?” — checking a specific permission against a specific resource, e.g. does usr_9821 have document:write on org_1123.
Authentication (AuthN) and Authorization (AuthZ) are essential parts of application security. Authentication verifies a user’s identity by answering the question, “Who are you?”, using methods such as passwords, multi-factor authentication (MFA), OIDC, or SAML. It usually occurs during login or token validation. Authorization determines what an authenticated user is allowed to access by answering, “What are you allowed to do?”. It is checked whenever a user requests a protected resource and uses mechanisms such as OAuth 2.0 scopes, Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), or Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC). In simple terms, authentication confirms identity, while authorization controls access based on roles and permissions.
How this goes wrong
A common failure: check that the JWT is valid, then let the request through, assuming a verified identity implies permission. In a multi-tenant system this produces Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA) — the user is who they say they are, but they were never entitled to touch that specific object. AuthN and AuthZ need to be two distinct checks: validate identity first, then explicitly evaluate the authenticated user’s permissions against the resource being requested.
3. The Threat Landscape

Press enter or click to view image in full size

This diagram illustrates a hierarchical classification of common authentication security threats, grouped into credential attacks, session/token attacks, and client-side/browser attacks.
You can’t design defenses without first mapping what you’re defending against.
Brute force. An attacker hammers one account with a password dictionary. Defend with progressive delays, temporary lockouts after repeated failures (e.g. 5 failures → 15-minute lock), and Redis-backed sliding-window rate limits by IP.
Credential stuffing. Attackers replay username/password pairs leaked from unrelated breaches, via botnets, against your login endpoint. Defend by checking new passwords against breach databases (HaveIBeenPwned’s k-anonymity API is the standard approach) and requiring MFA so a correct password alone isn’t enough.
Password spraying. Rather than hammering one account, the attacker tries one common password across thousands of accounts, staying under any single account’s lockout threshold. Defend with rate limits that track unique usernames in aggregate, geo-velocity checks, and a denylist of common passwords enforced globally, not per-account.
Session hijacking and token theft. An attacker exfiltrates a live session token — e.g. via a malicious browser extension reading local storage — and impersonates the user without ever seeing a password. Defend by never storing tokens anywhere JavaScript can read them: use HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookies exclusively.
Replay attacks. A valid, single-use artifact (a signed request, a reset link) is intercepted and resent. Defend with high-entropy nonces and strict single-use, short-lived tokens on every stateful flow.
CSRF. A malicious site tricks a logged-in user’s browser into firing an authenticated request — e.g. a hidden auto-submitting form posting to a bank transfer endpoint, riding on the browser’s automatically attached session cookie. Defend with SameSite=Lax/Strict cookies, and for legacy clients, custom headers or explicit CSRF tokens.
XSS. An attacker gets a script into a page other users will load — a <script> payload dropped into a profile field, executing in every visitor's browser and exfiltrating their cookies. Defend with context-aware output escaping, a strict Content Security Policy, and HttpOnly cookies so a successful injection still can't read the session token.
Clickjacking. A transparent iframe of your app sits invisibly over a page the user actually wants to click, so their real clicks land on your app’s buttons — a classic version overlays a “delete account” button under a fake prize claim. Defend with X-Frame-Options: DENY or Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'none'.
OAuth abuse and refresh token theft. An attacker intercepts an authorization code or a refresh token to gain long-lived, persistent API access without ever needing user consent. Defend by requiring PKCE on every client-side and native OAuth flow, and by enforcing strict refresh token rotation.
4. Core Security Principles
Zero trust. Nothing is implicitly trusted — not client input, not client storage, not “internal” network traffic. Every request is authenticated and authorized explicitly, every time.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

This diagram illustrates a layered authentication security pipeline, where every incoming request passes through multiple protection layers before securely accessing the database.
Least privilege. Components and users get exactly the access their job requires and nothing more. The auth service reads and writes user tables; it has no business touching invoice or document tables.
Defense in depth. No single control should be load-bearing. If the firewall is bypassed, validation middleware should catch it. If that’s bypassed, database-level checks should. If the database itself leaks, it should contain hashed and encrypted values, not plaintext.
Secure by default. New accounts start unverified. New endpoints are private unless explicitly marked public. The default state is always the more restrictive one.
Fail securely. An error during validation should deny access, not silently grant it:
export async function authMiddleware(
request: FastifyRequest,
reply: FastifyReply
) {
const token = request.headers.authorization?.replace("Bearer ", ""); try {
const payload = jwt.verify(token!, process.env.JWT_SECRET!);
if (!payload) {
return reply.status(401).send({
message: "Unauthorized",
});
}
} catch (error) {
}
}
Every unexpected exception in an auth check should resolve to “unauthorized,” not to falling through to proceed().
Assume breach. Design as though the database will eventually be read by someone who shouldn’t have it. Hash passwords with an expensive, memory-hard algorithm; encrypt sensitive fields like MFA secrets and phone numbers at rest; hash every token and session identifier before it touches the database.

5. High-Level Architecture
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This diagram illustrates the end-to-end authentication service architecture, showing how client requests flow securely through the API Gateway, Fastify service layers, validation, business logic, repositories, and caching before reaching the PostgreSQL and Redis data stores.Layers and it’s Responsibility
Client: Keeps the access token in memory only; sends the refresh token automatically via an HttpOnly cookie scoped to /auth/refresh.
API Gateway: The single entry point: TLS termination, rate limiting, and routing /auth/* into the isolated service. Forwards client IP and User-Agent downstream via headers like X-Forwarded-For.
Authentication Service: An isolated, lightweight container (e.g. Fastify) built for high-throughput reque
